techniques.evidence_quote: Pivoting on the unique URL path `/app/pages/bill.php` using URLScan, we were able to find several other kits deployed over the last 11 months, across 20 domains.
The attacker has compromised credentials, but they don't know where they've landed in the breached environment.
We observed an attacker attempting and failing to enumerate the settings for sending SMS messages (`GetSMSAttributes`) in 10 AWS regions over a period of five minutes

we were able to determine that it was running a phishing campaign impersonating the French government and had successfully phished user PII and credit card information. We confirmed this through a world-readable text file that had been left on the web server.

One of the notable things we observed was the use of Telegram to send users' PII, credit card details, and other request metadata to an attacker-controlled channel. This function was found in the `/server/config.php` file, which also contained Telegram API tokens.

Attackers with the correct permissions will enumerate SMS settings with `GetSMSAttributes` or `GetSMSSandboxAccountStatus` and then use the AWS SNS API call `Publish` to send out SMS messages with their phishing links.

attack_chain.description: Attacker obtained stolen AWS long-term IAM access keys (AKIA prefix) through an unspecified prior compromise, gaining initial access to the victim AWS environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker has compromised credentials, but they don't know where they've landed in the breached environment.

attack_chain.description: Attacker called `sts:GetCallerIdentity` to identify the compromised account and understand what credentials they held.

Attacker enumerated SMS sending capabilities via `sns:GetSMSAttributes` and `sns:GetSMSSandboxAccountStatus` across 10 AWS regions in approximately five minutes to assess the account's SMS sending potential and sandbox status.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: We observed an attacker attempting and failing to enumerate the settings for sending SMS messages (`GetSMSAttributes`) in 10 AWS regions over a period of five minutes

attack_chain.description: Attacker intended to use `sns:Publish` to send smishing (SMS phishing) messages via the victim's AWS account, abusing its SMS-sending capacity to distribute phishing links at scale. (Enumeration attempts failed in this specific case but the technique is the intended next step per attacker pattern.)
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Attackers with the correct permissions will enumerate SMS settings with `GetSMSAttributes` or `GetSMSSandboxAccountStatus` and then use the AWS SNS API call `Publish` to send out SMS messages with their phishing links.

attack_chain.description: Using the same IP infrastructure, the attacker hosted phishing sites impersonating the French government for payment of fines across 20 domains over 11 months.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Pivoting on the unique URL path `/app/pages/bill.php` using URLScan, we were able to find several other kits deployed over the last 11 months, across 20 domains.

attack_chain.description: Victims visiting the phishing site impersonating a French government fine-payment portal submitted their PII and credit card details, which were captured by the phishing kit.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: we were able to determine that it was running a phishing campaign impersonating the French government and had successfully phished user PII and credit card information. We confirmed this through a world-readable text file that had been left on the web server.

attack_chain.description: Captured victim PII, credit card details, and request metadata were exfiltrated in real-time to an attacker-controlled Telegram channel via the Telegram Bot API, using hardcoded tokens in `/server/config.php`.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: One of the notable things we observed was the use of Telegram to send users' PII, credit card details, and other request metadata to an attacker-controlled channel. This function was found in the `/server/config.php` file, which also contained Telegram API tokens.

initial_access_vector: Stolen long-term IAM access keys (AKIA prefix) obtained through an unspecified prior credential compromise
persistence_mechanism: Long-lived IAM access keys (AKIA prefix) provided persistent access without requiring re-authentication
